reforms.description: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 introduced significant reforms to enhance the accuracy of corporate financial statements and established a new oversight body for accountants, aiming to restore investor confidence in financial reporting.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By improving the reliability of financial reporting, the reform aims to enhance investor confidence, which can lead to increased investment and economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act was signed into law in 2002.
reforms.importance_rationale: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act represents a major overhaul of corporate governance and financial reporting standards, addressing systemic issues revealed by high-profile corporate scandals.
reforms.source_quote: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 created a new oversight body to supervise accountants, tightened the responsibility of corporate officers for financial reports and stiffened penalties.
